coredata.dc:title: Localized states induced by uniaxial strain in graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Fei-Fei
Liu, Zheng-Fang
Wu, Qing-Ping
Li, Wan-Ying
Xiao, Xian-Bo
coredata.dc:description: The construction of localized states has been one of the hot research topics in recent years. In this paper, an effective method of constructing the localized states (corner state, edge state and domain wall state) in graphene quantum dots is proposed theoretically by using the uniaxial strain effect. The research shows that the distribution characteristics and forms of these localized states are closely related to the uniaxial strain direction. Specifically, the formation of these localized states is mainly attributed to the inconsistent hopping amplitude caused by the uniaxial strain, which makes graphene quantum dots become an extended 2 D Su–Schrieffer–Heeger (SSH) model. It must be emphasized that the formation of these local states only requires the effect of strain, and all of them are in the band gap of the system, so they are very easy to be directly observed and used as single-channel quantum wires. Then, we propose a method to analyze the localized states by building a two-dimensional extended SSH model through a one-dimensional SSH chain. The theoretical speculation of our proposed method is highly consistent with the simulated local states phenomenon induced by uniaxial strain applied to the monolayer graphene. This method of constructing the localized states by using uniaxial strain to realize the extended two-dimensional SSH model is not only suitable for electronic crystals like graphene, but also for artificial crystals like photonic crystals and acoustic crystals. Our findings provide a new ideas for circuit design for quantum computing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Localized states
Strain
SSH model
Graphene

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The band gap of the monolayer graphene
The localized states of rhomboidal quantum dots
The localized states of rectangle graphene quantum dots

The red circle marks two zero-energy states. There are density of states of the zero-energy degenerate localized state of the diamond-shaped monolayer graphene as shown in the inset. ). There are schematic of the rectangle monolayer graphene as shown in the inset. (b) Density of states of the non-zero energy degenerate localized state of the rectangle monolayer graphene. (c) Schematic of the rectangle model. (d) Energy spectrum of the rectangle model. (e)

of states of the non-zero energy localized state of the rectangle model with adding an identical atomic chain. The
| ψ |

profiles marked red and green are identical.

head.ce:title: Localized states induced by uniaxial strain in graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, East China Jiaotong University, Nanchang, 330013, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics, East China Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, East China Jiaotong University, Nanchang, 330013, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Computer, Jiangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanchang, 330004, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Computer, Jiangxi University of Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, East China Jiaotong University, Nanchang, 330013, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of localized states has been one of the hot research topics in recent years. In this paper, an effective method of constructing the localized states (corner state, edge state and domain wall state) in graphene quantum dots is proposed theoretically by using the uniaxial strain effect. The research shows that the distribution characteristics and forms of these localized states are closely related to the uniaxial strain direction. Specifically, the formation of these localized states is mainly attributed to the inconsistent hopping amplitude caused by the uniaxial strain, which makes graphene quantum dots become an extended 2 D Su–Schrieffer–Heeger (SSH) model. It must be emphasized that the formation of these local states only requires the effect of strain, and all of them are in the band gap of the system, so they are very easy to be directly observed and used as single-channel quantum wires. Then, we propose a method to analyze the localized states by building a two-dimensional extended SSH model through a one-dimensional SSH chain. The theoretical speculation of our proposed method is highly consistent with the simulated local states phenomenon induced by uniaxial strain applied to the monolayer graphene. This method of constructing the localized states by using uniaxial strain to realize the extended two-dimensional SSH model is not only suitable for electronic crystals like graphene, but also for artificial crystals like photonic crystals and acoustic crystals. Our findings provide a new ideas for circuit design for quantum computing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the realization of local states such as corner states, edge states and domain wall states using various systems and modes has become a research hotspot [1–20]. For example, in the modified Haldane model, corner states and edge states [2] are found, but the emergence of these local states requires a dimerization term and parameters with many conditions to realize. The Haldane model is not an actual crystal rather than the artificial model. Corner states and edge states are also found in a quantized acoustic octupole topological insulator [3]. Negative coupling resonators are required to achieve such local states. If the long-range interaction is considered, the corner state can also be realized in the photonic Kagomi crystal [10]. Domain wall states have been constructed in a large number of systems, such as: the coupled dimer chain [12], the twisted Kitaev chain [13], the antiferromagnetic coupled double lattice system [14], the graphene based staggered sublattice potential junction [21], etc. When partially filled with a midgapped band, the edge states can behave like a one-dimensional metal at the edge of a semiconductor and may be used as a single-channel quantum wire. Similarly, the domain wall states behaves like a one-dimensional metal embedded in a semiconductor. Domain wall states can be used for creating a perfect valley filter [21] and a single-channel quantum wire [19].

In addition, graphene has also been extensively studied and explored in recent years [1,19,21–26]. The construction of graphene-based local states has always been one of the hot topics at present. However, graphene is a zero band gap semiconductor, which is unfavorable for practical applications [23]. The method of opening a gap in the spectrum of the hexagonal graphene is to break the symmetry of two triangular sublattices. This can be achieved by introducing staggered field energy [21], multilayer graphene [16], the deformation of bonds on graphene lattice [26,27], an in-plane Zeeman field [9], and a magnetic field [28]. In this paper, uniaxial strain is used to open the energy gap of graphene. This is mainly due to the excellent mechanical properties of graphene [29]. Uniaxial strain makes the hopping amplitude of the monolayer graphene different by making the bond length of the monolayer graphene different in three directions, thus opening the band gap. Although the influence of applied uniaxial strain have been analyzed and operated theoretically and experimentally [22,26,27,30], using uniaxial strain to construct the localized states has not been deeply discussed before. We theoretically analyzed the existence principle of local states in graphene with uniaxial strain. After applying appropriate uniaxial strain, local states such as corner state, edge state, and domain wall state can be obtained easily by changing the direction of uniaxial strain without any other mathematical or physical assumptions, and these local states are all in the band gap. We provide a new idea for constructing localized states in a simple way. The unequal hopping amplitude in the monolayer graphene realizes the staggered hopping amplitude of different atoms in a single graphene atom chain. The model can be extended from one dimension to two dimensions and keep its edge characteristics unchanged [31]. Therefore, the monolayer graphene with the strain is applied can be regarded as one-dimensional atomic chains with staggered hopping amplitude superposed along a fixed direction, which is very similar to the structure of an SSH model. On this basis, we propose a method to analyze the relationship between the local state in the simplified model constructed by SSH chain and local states in the graphene with uniaxial strain. The rationality of the proposed method was demonstrated by building different shape models. The results show that the localized state phenomenon in graphene monolayer under uniaxial strain is basically consistent with that calculated by simplified SSH chain. It reasonably explains the localized states in the monolayer graphene, and further verifies the correctness of the analytical method. The localized states realized by uniaxial strain in the monolayer graphene provides a new idea for the further application of the graphene in the electronic field.

The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 describes the principle of applying uniaxial strain to open the band gap. The zero-energy corner states, the zero-energy zigzag edge states and the non-zero energy domain walls states in the two-dimensional diamond-shaped monolayer graphene are introduced in Section 3. Furthermore, the method of calculating the range of edge states is introduced. This chapter studies in detail how to use SSH to build a rhomboidal model and proves that it can reasonably explain the principle of the localized states in the monolayer graphene with uniaxial strain. In Section 4, we discuss in detail the zero-energy armchair edge state and the non-zero energy domain wall state in two-dimensional rectangular monolayer graphene. Finally, we conclude in Section 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The band gap of the monolayer graphene
ce:section.ce:para:
The Hamiltonian after applying uniaxial strain to the monolayer graphene can be generally described as [22,32]:
(1)
H = ∑
∑

t

i j

+ H . c .
where
c
i
†
and
c
j
are creation and annihilation field operators respectively, for an electron on sublattices A and B in the monolayer graphene. Here i , j denotes a position on the Bravais lattice.
i , j
denotes the set of all nearest neighbors,
t
i j
is the corresponding nearest neighbor hopping parameter.
(2)
ϵ = ɛ

Here, ɛ is the tensile strain. Poisson’s ratio of graphite:
σ = 0 . 165
. In the uniaxial strained monolayer graphene, the deformed bond lengths according to the equation Eq. (2) are given by

. The change of bond length of the monolayer graphene in three directions leads to the change of hoppings amplitudes in the corresponding direction:
t

0

− 3 . 37

− 1 )
, where

i
is the bond length with strain, i
= 1,2,3. Here
t
0
≈ 2 . 7
eV
. The relationship between the uniaxial strain direction angle and the hoppings amplitudes in three different directions with strain is shown in Fig. 1(b). Meanwhile different hoppings amplitudes can form a one-dimensional SSH chain, such as the zigzag edge represented by a and the armchair edge represented by b which both represented by red shaded rectangles in Fig. 1(a). There is a band gap in the spectrum of the monolayer graphene under uniaxial strain, as long as the following conditions are met [32],
(3)
|
|
t
1
|
|
t
2
|
± 1 |
>
|
t
3
|
|
t
2
|
The threshold of uniaxial strain opening band gap of monolayer graphene is independent of the shape of the monolayer graphene model. In order to ensure the localized states phenomenon in the band gap is obvious, the fixed strain (
ɛ = 0 . 24
in Figs. 4 and 7,
ɛ = 0 . 27
in the other figures) is used in this paper. The literature [33] shows that silicon doped graphene can open a useful band gap by applying strain. The bond energy of CSi bond is smaller, and the strain it can bear is lower than that of CC bond, with a maximum strain of 0.22. The inherent tensile strength of graphene is
130
GPa
, which can withstand the non-destructive deformation of
25 % − 27 %
of the failure strain [29,34,35]. When the applied strain along the
θ = 0
and
1
3
π
direction is 0.27, the bond length deformation (
δ
i
) is within 20%, so it can be realized theoretically. It is worth noting that when the strain is applied in the direction of the armchair
θ =
1
2
π
(seeing Figs. 4 and 7),
ɛ = 0 . 24
is selected, which leads to the bond length deformation is 24%.

The SSH model describes spinless fermions hopping on a one-dimensional lattice (chain) with staggered hopping amplitudes [36]. In SSH model, each cell carries two sublattice positions one of type A and the other of type B. There are two kinds of bipartite hopping amplitudes, including the intracell hopping within the same cell w and the intercell hopping v to the nearest-neighbor cell as shown in Fig. 1(e)–(f) [36,37]. The SSH chain consists of N unit cells. The SSH bulk momentum-space hamiltonian can be written
(4)
H
( k )
=
0
h
( k )
h
∗
( k )
0
;
h
( k )
= v + w cos k − i w sin k
We already know that the topological properties of one-dimensional SSH chain can be judged by solving the winding number [36–39]. The Chern number [36,37,39,40] is suitable for two-dimensional and above systems. The winding number of the SSH chain in Fig. 1(e)–(f) can be calculated by
(5)
τ =
1
2 π i
∫
− π
π
d k
d
d k
log h
( k )

Through calculating the equation Eq. (5), the winding number of the SSH chain in Fig. 1(e) is 1, that is, the SSH chain is topological. When v is greater than w, the SSH chain is topological. It is worth noting that there are two isolated 0 -dimensional localized states (corner states) at the left and right ends of SSH model. Similarly, according to the equation Eq. (5), the winding number of the SSH chain in Fig. 1(f) is 0, that is, the SSH chain is non-topological. When v is smaller than w, the SSH chain is non-topological. When the SSH model is superimposed along a fixed direction, a two-dimensional extended SSH model is obtained. Its topological properties can be known by calculating the Chern number [36,37,39,40]. The Chern numbers of the nth band on a discretized Brillouin zone is defined by
(6)
c
n
=
1
2 π i
∫
T
2

2
k
F
12
( k )
where the Berry connection
A
μ
( k )
( = 1 , 2 )
and the associated field strength
F
12
( k )
are given by
(7)
A
μ
( k )
=
〈 n
( k )

∂

( k )

1
( k )

( k )

( k )
〉
.

In addition, the shape of monolayer graphene is closely related to the appearance of the localized states. The only way to limit the graphene’s spatial shape is to cut the spatial shape of the graphene. The relationship between the shape and the localized states is explored by discussing two different shapes of graphene: rhomboidal and rectangle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The localized states of rhomboidal quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The case of the diamond-shaped monolayer graphene quantum dot with the fixed uniaxial strain is first discussed in Fig. 2. We first consider the case of uniaxial strain along the x -axis (
θ = 0 ,
δ
1
= 1 . 19 ,
δ
2
= 0 . 95 ,
δ
3
= 1 . 19
). According to the energy spectrum obtained from the Hamiltonian with strain, it can be seen that there are two zero-energy degenerate localized states located in the energy gap in Fig. 2(b). The density of states diagram (subgraph of Fig. 2(b)) shows that the zero-energy localized state is the corner state. When the uniaxial strain is applied along the x -axis direction (
t
2
>
t
1
=
t
3
) in Fig. 1(b) ,
t
2
and
t
3
of the P edge are same as
t
2
and
t
1
of the Q edge. In fact, the P edge and Q edge are topological because the hopping amplitudes
t
2
is greater than that
t
1
=
t
3
. So the corner states is topological. Because each SSH (the P edge and Q edge) chain in the diamond-shaped monolayer graphene produces two isolated point, and the upper and lower ends of the diamond are superposed to form two corner states in the band gap.

For the purpose of understanding the formation of corner states more clearly, we use the method of dimension reduction. Since the bulk band gap (i.e. bulk insulation) is obtained after the uniaxial strain applied to the monolayer graphene, only the edge localized states are considered. We build a rhomboidal model by connecting the head and tail of four identical SSH chains (Fig. 2(c)). The SSH chain is divided into topological and non-topological cases. In this case, the SSH chain is topological. We can know from the energy spectrum that there are two zero-energy localized states in Fig. 2(c). Since the Hamiltonian of SSH model obeys chiral symmetry
H
ˆ
Γ
ˆ
= −
Γ
ˆ
H
ˆ
, any energy exists in pairs with opposite energy [36], that is, the localized states of zero-energy appear in pairs (degenerate), where
Γ
ˆ
is the chiral symmetry operator.
| ψ |
2
of two zero-energy localized states show the zero-energy localized states are localized at the intersections (Fig. 2(d)). When the SSH chain is topological, the corner state is topological, and when the SSH chain is non-topological, the corner state is also non-topological. The distribution of the corner state does not change in the topological case where
t
1
is greater than
t
3
under a certain range (
θ ∈
0 , 0 . 13 π
) of strain. The corner distribution of the diamond-shaped monolayer graphene is consistent with the conclusion of the rhomboidal model obtained by the method introduced.

Similarly, we analyzed the case
θ =
1
3
π
(
t
1
>
t
2
=
t
3
,
δ
1
= 0 . 95 ,
δ
2
= 1 . 19 ,
δ
3
= 1 . 19
) in Fig. 1(b) . In fact, the distribution of local states of diamond-shaped monolayer graphene remains unchanged in
0 . 22 π , 0 . 44 π
. There are the zero-energy localized states in the energy spectrum and the density of states diagram shows the zero-energy localized state is the edge state in the upper left corner of Fig. 3(b).

The monolayer graphene with strain can be seen as extending from one-dimensional staggered hopping energy atom chain to one two-dimensional in a fixed direction, and keeping its edge characteristics unchanged [31]. We combined the SSH chains Q side by side along the P direction [6,31] with a single inter-chain hopping amplitude
t
1
, and intra-chain hopping amplitudes are staggered with values
t
2
and
t
3
as shown in Fig. 3(a). When θ is
1
3
π
, for edge Q and edge P (the zigzag edges), the edge states is not zero.
t
1
far greater than
t
2
of the Q edge, therefore the Q edge is topological. The isolated points are formed at the left end and the right end of the Q edge respectively. Each chain parallel to the Q edge is superposed along the P direction to form the edge state on the P edge.

The one-dimensional edge state also will be localized at the edge Q in the lower right corner of Fig. 3(b) when the applied strain direction is negative. It should be noted that the energy spectrum has not changed. According to this phenomenon, we can control the position of the edge states by controlling the uniaxial strain direction angle.

In addition, domain walls will appear when the direction angle of uniaxial strain is
1
2
π
(armchair directions,
δ
1
= 1 . 03 ,
δ
2
= 1 , 24 ,
δ
3
= 1 . 03
). The edge P and the edge Q are the same SSH chains because
t
1
=
t
3
>
t
2
in Fig. 1(b). It is worth noting that
t
1
is equal to
t
3
and not equal to
t
2
in the diamond-shaped monolayer graphene when the strain is applied along the x -axis and the y -axis. The difference is that
t
2
is larger than
t
1
(
θ = 0
) but less than
t
1
(
θ =
1
2
π
). At this case, the top is no longer a single isolated quantum dot, but a trimer quantum dot. There are the zero-energy degenerate and non-zero energy localized states in the energy spectrum in Fig. 4(a). The density of states diagram in Fig. 4(b) shows that the non-zero energy localized states is the domain wall states.
t
1
is greater than
t
2
, so the domain wall is non-topological.

To directly observe the distribution of the localized states, only the case where the band gap can be opened is considered. Through the analysis of topology and non-topology cases, the band gap exists only in non-topological cases(
θ =
1
2
π
). To explain the emergence of the domain wall states, the origin of zero-energy degenerate and non-zero energy non-degenerate states is discussed by constructing models. Firstly we divide the diamond into two triangles symmetrical about the x -axis. In addition to the degenerate zero-energy states, we pay more attention to the existence of non-zero energy non-degenerate states near them (Fig. 4(a)). By studying the probability distribution of non-zero energy local states, it is found that they are distributed at the interface of the upper and lower parts. For each triangle, its interior is a one-dimensional monatomic chain arranged along the x -axis. A triangle model consisting of two SSH chains (the edge Q) and one atomic chain is used to simulate the phenomenon (Fig. 4(c)). Through the energy spectrum distribution diagram of the triangle model in Fig. 4(d), there is indeed a double degenerate zero-energy local states. The
| ψ |
2
of two zero-energy localized states shows that they are corner states and localized states of zero-energy degenerate, respectively (Fig. 4(e)). The degenerated zero-energy state is formed by the degenerated one-dimensional atomic chain. The accuracy of the theory is verified by simulating the triangle model with different atomic chains. Adding an atomic chain on the basis of the triangle model above (Fig. 4(f)) does not destroy the translational symmetry, so there are three zero-energy degenerate localized states in the energy spectrum in Fig. 4(g). Three zero-energy states are distributed on three atomic chains. In Fig. 4(h),
| ψ |
2
of states diagram shows the zero-energy degenerate localized state are the localized state and corner state. In order to further explain the origin of the non-zero energy interface state, we add an atomic chain with inversion symmetry on the basis of the above figure (Fig. 4(c)). On the basis of the triangular model, add an atomic chain that is axially symmetric with the original atomic chain about x , and the translational symmetry of the model is broken in Fig. 5(a). The localized states are non-zero energy non-degenerate in Fig. 5(b).
| ψ |
2
of states diagram in Fig. 5(c) shows that the non-zero energy localized state is the interface state and zero-energy localized state is the corner state. The addition of atomic chains identical to the primitive atomic chains and the atomic chains symmetric about the x -axis do not make either of the corner state or the interface state disappear. It is found from the energy spectrum that the previously degenerated zero-energy state is split due to the addition of an inverted symmetric atomic chain. It is concluded that the degenerate state of non-zero energy originates from the inversion symmetry of the system, because this inversion symmetry destroys the direct translational symmetry of the atomic chain. Two identical triangular models symmetrically superpose on the x -axis to form a rhomboidal model, and the superposed region forms the domain wall states. Therefore, the domain wall states appear in the two-dimensional diamond-shaped monolayer graphene with strain (
θ =
1
2
π
).

By controlling the direction angle of uniaxial strain applied on the diamond-shaped monolayer graphene, we realized three different localized states, namely, zero-energy corner state, zero-energy edge state and non-zero energy domain wall state. Except we also change strain direction to control the location of the local distribution of zero-energy edge state. The proposed dimension reduction interpretation method is in perfect agreement with the actual local state distribution. To further verify the accuracy of the realization and interpretation of the local state method, the next chapter further discusses the localized states of the rectangle monolayer graphene with strain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The localized states of rectangle graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
At present, it is still difficult to realize the edge state of armchair in most models. In this chapter, we solve this problem in a relatively simple way, that is, using uniaxial strain to deform the rectangle monolayer graphene. The armchair edge states can be achieved by applying a range (
0 . 16 π , 0 . 44 π
) of uniaxial strain deviated from the x -axis in the rectangular monolayer graphene in Fig. 6(a). The distribution of energy spectrum and the localized states are similar in this range. With the increase of the angle, the non-zero energy bulk states is more and more degenerated into the zero-energy localized states. For convenience of observation, the simulation data of
θ = 0 . 4 π
is selected (
δ
1
= 0 . 96 ,
δ
2
= 1 . 24 ,
δ
3
= 1 . 12
).

According to the relationship between the direction angle of uniaxial strain and the hopping energy in Fig. 1(b),
t
1
is larger than
t
3
and
t
2
(
t
1
>
t
2
≈
t
3
). We stacked the M edge (hopping amplitudes of the SSH chain are staggered with values
t
1
and
t
3
) along the direction of the N edge (inter-chain hopping amplitude
t
2
) to build the rectangular monolayer graphene. The M edge chains stacked in the N edge direction are not identical to each other. Only chains with the same parity of M chain serial number have the same stacking mode. According to the dimension reduction principle [31], the localized states of the M edge is the same as that of the rectangular monolayer graphene.
t
1
is larger than
t
3
of the M edge, so the M edge is topological. In Fig. 6(a), the energy spectrum of the rectangle monolayer graphene shows that there are the zero-energy degenerate localized states in the band gap. The density of states diagram shows that the zero-energy degenerate localized state is the armchair edge state in Fig. 6(b). Since the M edge is topological, the M edge chain of odd layers forms an isolated state at the left end. On the contrary, the M edge chain of even layers forms an isolated state at the right end. These isolated states are superposed layer by layer along the N edge direction to form the armchair edge states. The armchair edge states is topological.

When the direction angle of uniaxial strain applied in rectangular monolayer graphene is
1
2
π
(armchair directions,
δ
1
= 1 . 03 ,
δ
2
= 1 . 24 ,
δ
3
= 1 . 03
), there are the non-zero energy degenerate localized states located in the band gap in Fig. 7(a). Here we discuss the phenomenon of rectangular model when the SSH chain is non-topological (
t
1
>
t
3
). The density of states diagram in Fig. 7(b) shows that the non-zero energy degenerate localized state is the domain wall states. Since
t
1
=
t
3
, the M edge is equivalent to one-dimensional atomic chain. A rectangular model constructed by two identical SSH chains and two atomic chains (Fig. 7(c)). In Fig. 7(d), the energy spectrum of the rectangle model shows that there are two zero-energy degenerate localized states in the band gap.
| ψ |
2
of states shows that the zero-energy degenerate localized states both are the interface states in Fig. 7(e). Adding an atomic chain on the basis of the rectangle model (Fig. 7(f)), there are three zero-energy degenerate localized states in Fig. 7(g). From
| ψ |
2
of states in Fig. 7(h), the zero-energy degenerate localized states both are the interface states. By adding the same atomic chain, the translational symmetry is maintained, and the further inversion symmetry is maintained, so there are the degenerate zero-energy states. Because edge M is based on the particularity of odd even stacking, rectangular graphene can be seen as two identical rectangular graphene formed with respect to the x -axis symmetry, so domain walls are formed at the interface of the symmetry axis. By studying the density diagram of degenerated non-zero energy local states, it is found that the domain wall states do indeed distribute at each symmetric interface. This phenomenon is completely consistent with the phenomenon predicted by the method.

The armchair zero-energy edge states and non-zero energy degenerate domain wall states of the rectangular monolayer graphene with the uniaxial strain are introduced in detail. The local state distribution obtained by dimension reduction interpretation method is consistent with that in rectangular graphene, which further verifies the realization of local state in rectangular monolayer graphene under uniaxial strain and the correctness of interpretation method.

Four different localized states (corner states, zigzag and armchair edge states, and domain wall states) are realized by a relatively simple method, namely, only applying uniaxial strain on the monolayer graphene. And we can control the local position of the edge state by controlling the direction angle of uniaxial strain in the diamond-shaped monolayer graphene. To explain the above four localized states phenomena in the two-dimensional model, a new way is proposed. We get enlightenment from the reduced dimension
S S H
model and propose to build a two-dimensional model through the extended
S S H
model. And a rigorous demonstration is made through the numerical simulation of the monolayer graphene. Through two different models of the rhomboidal and the rectangle, the relationship between the different shapes of the model and the localized states is discussed. This method can not only be used to analyze the localized states of the monolayer graphene, but also be applied to one-dimensional quantum wells and phononic crystals. The different localized states of the monolayer graphene induced by uniaxial strain provides a new idea for further realizing topological phenomena in a simple way. It adds new possibilities for the practical application of the monolayer graphene.
